Functions.title: Intro to Functions
Functions.description: Master the basics of functions in programming. Enhance your coding skills with clear examples and practical insights. Perfect for beginners!
Functions.transcript:
[00:00] Hey, welcome to the functions exercise of the workshop. This one is great. You're going to use functions everywhere. So far, we've got programs that will run line by line. Sometimes they'll skip lines, sometimes they'll repeat lines, but pretty much they run top to bottom and that's it.

[00:16] Now we're going to be able to take a section of that program, put it in a special place, and then our program as it's running can go over there for a second, go do stuff, and then come back, and then go over there, do stuff, and come back. And in fact this is really cool, we're not actually going to cover this in the exercise, but you're going to have chunks of code and inside of that can be references to other functions that are chunks of code. So this can go all the way down. This would be like go into here and now go into here and go into here. This is actually what we call the call stack.

[00:45] And so as it goes down, we're stacking these calls. And when it's done, it returns. And then it comes back to this one, and it continues. And it goes back to this, continues. And then maybe it goes into this one and continues.

[00:55] It's pretty interesting. And we're using functions all through all of that. So functions are a really critical part of programming in general, and JavaScript in particular. So let's explore a little bit about the syntax of this, and then you'll jump right into the exercise. So here is a basic TypeScript function.

[01:13] There's the function keyword, the name of the function, some parentheses, the arguments that it's going to accept, the parameters that we're going to pass. And so here we've got a is a number and b is a number. We need to have those types. And then you have what we call the return type, so what we're expecting to return from the function. That is a number.

[01:32] We're going to return a plus b. Okay, pretty straightforward. That makes sense. That return keyword is important there. These braces are important there.

[01:38] And this is what we call a function declaration. We'll explore that versus some other ways that you can write functions as well. So, yep, talked about this is maybe a little more spaces there. There you go. That's a parameter.

[01:51] This is the name. We probably could specify that. But this is the TypeScript portion specifically. And then here's a return type. And yeah, so you might wonder why do we need to type the functions?

[02:03] Why do we need to specify this? Hopefully by now you've kind of gotten used to how TypeScript can help with things. But here if we had a function without types called process, we don't know what data is. We don't get any TypeScript help from data. And so for the same reason we want to type of variables, we also want to type our inputs.

[02:22] It just makes it much more clear what we can do with the data that we're receiving. And on the other end of things, people are going to be calling our functions, they're going to be passing things to our functions, and they might have variables or expressions they want to pass to it, and they want to be warned and compilation to fail if they are not passing the right thing to our function. Because if we're expecting an array that has a length property, and we pass a, I was gonna say a string, but a string actually also has a length property. If we pass it a number, then that's going to blow up in our faces because numbers don't have a length property. And then also the return value is important as well because if we call process with something and we get something back and we try to call a string method on it when it's actually supposed to return a number that'll blow up and so by having these types specified it makes things a lot more clear and TypeScript can help us with that.

[03:16] TypeScript can infer some things. We're going to have an exercise about that as well. So we've got our function, and you'll notice it is returning a number here, but we don't have a return type like we have in the previous example. And yeah, that's because TypeScript is able to look at all the returns in our function, which by the way you can have multiple returns. You have if this, then return that, otherwise return that.

[03:39] So you can have multiple return statements, but it looks at each one of those return statements And from that it derives what the return of this function could possibly be. And then we'll also talk a little bit about the forms that functions can take. And here this is called a function declaration. I prefer that typically. But arrow functions are also a format that you can use, and we'll look at that as well.

[04:06] And then finally, I don't actually really talk about this so much in this exercise because we haven't quite gotten to this point yet, but with arrays you have special methods that can accept functions, and it's typically easier to pass an arrow function to that. And we do have an example of passing a function to another function so you'll get a little bit of exposure to that which would be cool. And with that I think we are good so awesome let's just jump into oh also we do talk about documenting your functions, too. So look forward to that. OK, it's going to be great.

[04:38] Have a good time. We'll see you in a bit.

solution.title: Function Declaration
solution.transcript:
[00:00] Okay, let's make this function declaration. So we're going to make function called getMessage and return our value. And yep, AI knows exactly what to do here. So what is this? What are we looking at?

[00:12] This is called a function declaration. It starts with the keyword function. You give it a space to separate function from the name of the function, and then you give these parentheses. We'll talk about what those parentheses can be used for later, what can go in between them. And then we have this return value that we're specifying.

[00:29] So it's a colon and then the the type of value that we are returning. And then we have our block. This is what we actually call a closure in JavaScript. So variables defined in here will exist as long as this function exists. And it's a pretty interesting thing to consider with garbage collection, all this stuff that we're going to get into later.

[00:52] But inside of this closure we can do whatever we want to, but at the very end we're going to have a return statement that here we're saying hello functions and that has to match our return value. So if I save this, we're going to get our log because of this log, and then we'll see our export just a function called getMessage. And if we were to try to return number here, we're going to get a type error because that doesn't match our return value. If I change this to number, then now we're going to get that return value in return. And yeah, that is the very basics of our function.

[01:27] We've got lots more to talk about with functions. They're really powerful primitive of JavaScript or really any language being able to reuse this stuff and everything. So this is a very important part of learning how to develop software. And this is that piece. The cool thing about this too is we can do this as many times as we want to.

[01:50] And now we get hello functions. And then we can say, you know what? I want to be really excited about this. And so we just change it there once. And we get it all applied everywhere else that we're calling the function.

[02:02] That might look a little bit like a loop, and yeah, you could definitely use this in a loop. But the point is that you don't have to use it in a loop. You can call it the function from wherever that function is accessible. And I think that's pretty cool. So there you go.

[02:17] That is your first introduction to functions. Hello, functions.

Functions.title: Parameters
Functions.transcript:
[00:00] So now we want to do a little bit more than we were doing before. We want to accept what are called parameters, or arguments. [00:00] Okay, let's talk about different ways that you can write functions. We're going to get into arrow functions in this exercise. And there are actually a couple other ways that you can write functions. There's function expressions, and there's actually method syntax on objects and classes and stuff. We're not going to get into those, so we're not done.

[00:18] There's also generator functions and stuff, and async functions. We're not getting into every function type in this exercise. We're going to focus on function declarations, which we've done so far, and arrow functions. There are actually features of arrow functions that we're not going to dive too deep into either because they require knowledge of other things that we're going to get to later. So mostly this is going to be a syntactic difference between function declarations and arrow functions, but there will be a couple of semantic differences that we'll talk about here as well.

[00:50] So, have a good time with this one, and we'll see you when you're done.

solution.title: Arrow Functions
solution.transcript:
[00:00] So let's get right into it. We're going to convert this function into an arrow function with an implicit return. So what does that mean? So we're going to say const double, and I'm going to comment this out so we can compare, with the end number. So here are a couple things different already.

[00:17] So first of all, we're not using the function keyword. We're using const and then double. This is like any other variable that we've created. The difference here is what comes after the equal sign. And you'll notice function declarations don't have an equal sign, because they're not a variable assigned to a function they're a special type of variable that is created as a function declaration so in the case of arrow functions an arrow function itself is actually an expression rather than a statement which is what we're doing with the function declaration And so you can pass this as an argument to other functions.

[00:49] You can use it in a ternary. You can do all sorts of things in the process of creating an arrow function. And we'll definitely do that later on. So the name of the function is the name of the variable. It's going to accept our number so that part looks the same.

[01:05] It also returns a number and again we don't need to have that explicit return type because that can be inferred so let's get rid of that. And then we have this funny looking thing. It looks almost like it's saying equal to or greater than or typically you would write that greater than or equal to. With arrow functions it's the equal sign and then the greater than sign is how you write that And it is kind of funny. It looks a little bit interesting, but it's like an arrow pointing to this function does this.

[01:38] I don't know. It was common in other languages and so they added it in JavaScript in this way. It's just kind of interesting. So yeah, Then we do n times 2. You'll notice the lack of a return keyword, and that is because with an arrow function, if it does not have curly braces, then whatever the arrow is pointing to, it's going to be returned implicitly.

[02:01] That's just how they work. This makes them really nice for simple functions like this that are just a one-liner utility sort of function. If it does have curly braces then you can put it on multiple lines and you can still use a return and that will work exactly the same. And the benefit here being that you can do other things as well, whereas if you just do the implicit return, you literally cannot do anything except an expression. No statements allowed, just expressions.

[02:27] And so you'll sometimes see people make arrow functions and not use the explicit return because they want to have multiple statements going on inside of the body of that function. For myself, I don't do that. I don't write functions that look like this a whole lot. If I've got something like this, then I'm absolutely going to convert it to a function declaration. But there are situations where you'll have an arrow function that does have multiple lines, and I use an arrow function for that, which you will see later on.

[02:58] So let's just bring this back to a single one-liner function. Very nice for this type of thing. All right, great. Oh, one other thing before we go too far on this. One important difference between a function declaration and all the other types of ways that you can call functions is when you can actually call them.

[03:18] So I'm going to actually save this file. We're going to come down here to... Actually, you know what, we'll just add a console.log right here. We'll say console.log double five. Sweet.

[03:29] Okay, double five is ten. I comment this out, same... Oh! Oh, right, right. Comment this out.

[03:37] There we go. Okay, same thing in either case. So here's where the difference happens. Let's go with our function declaration. I'm gonna move this to before the function declaration.

[03:49] We're still getting 10. Okay, so that's a really cool thing about function declarations is that you can put them anywhere inside of the file and as long as they are in the same block as where they're being called. So like we couldn't put this inside of a block like this because now double is outside of the block where this is defined and so we don't have access to double. So we can move it inside. There we go, that would be fine.

[04:15] So I say that because sometimes you'll say if thing, then whatever and now we're outside of that block so when I say you can put it anywhere in the file what I mean is you can put it anywhere inside of the a given block and Anywhere else within that block you can call it from anywhere above below wherever Even inside actually don't do that that That's called recursion and that would break our program terribly. So we're not going to bother with that. We can talk about recursion later. OK, all of that said, you cannot do the same thing with a variable that is declared in this way, an expression, a function expression, or an arrow function like this. And so for this reason, I actually really prefer function declarations for things that are not like really basic utilities.

[05:06] Typically I'll just have my utilities up at the top and I don't need to worry about it, but yeah lots of the time you're going to find my functions are function declarations in large part because those mean that I don't have to care what order these variables are declared and initialized and everything. It's just nice not to have to worry about that. But in the case here, I would probably go with the arrow function just because it's a single line utility. I'm probably not going to use it up at the top, and it's going to work just fine. So there you go.

[05:37] That's a really unique aspect of function declarations and why I prefer them for a lot of the functions that I write. OK. Whew. Let's move on. So we're going to convert this into an arrow function.

[05:51] Same deal. So there we go. Yep, there's not really anything new there. We can add our return type there if we want to. Looks like the AI wanted to do that, so we're not going to do that.

[06:06] We're going to also convert this one to an arrow function. So calculate total. Now, this one has an explicit return because it has multiple lines. And it's not so much about the lines. You can actually have multiple lines.

[06:18] In fact, we could say, put that right there, and that would be technically correct. Now, Prettier, when I save this, is going to reformat it because it looks nicer to be on one line. But you can totally do this. And In fact, in React world, lots of times you will see, whoops, see things like this, where you have this parentheses right here, and a closing parentheses right here, and you don't need a return statement. In fact, in React land, this would be like div, div, and here's our div and our, you know, hello name thing, whatever.

[06:52] So this is all React stuff. Don't worry so much about it. And we're not in a TSX file, so it's not going to work anyway. But yeah, you'll see stuff like this a lot. And so anyway, useful bit of information, but because we are doing multiple statements, that's what forces us into using the curly braces.

[07:11] And now we have to have an explicit return. So there's our calculate total right there. We're going to make our isEven. Again, we don't necessarily need the return type. We have this applyToNumber.

[07:25] This takes a number and a transform function and returns a transformed number. So this is one of the really cool things about TypeScript and JavaScript in general that I really like and that is that functions are first-class citizens. What that means is they operate like they are objects. You can take a function and pass it to something else and use that inside of that function. So this one is really interesting.

[07:49] So apply to number, you take a value which is a number, and then you take a transform which is a function that takes the number and returns a number. And then you can say transform value. So whatever you passed me, you're going to be in charge of determining how this number is transformed. I'm just going to take the number and transform. I'm going to call that transform with that number.

[08:10] This is pretty cool because what it means is I can now create functions out of functions. So here I got my triple and I got my square and then I can apply to number 5 and I want to triple it I want to square it and with that you can save this and now we got triple of 5 is 15 and square of 5 is 25. So that's pretty neat. Functions being passed as parameters or arguments to another function. And then that function can be then used.

[08:44] And it composes together beautifully. And it's like a beautiful orchestral piece. I don't know why I said that. It composes together very nicely. But yeah, a little bit less related to arrow functions.

[08:57] We could definitely, in fact, my IDE can even convert this to a named function, And it's still going to work just as well. But where this becomes useful is in the typing, where you can type this as a function that returns a number. And so that's why it's included here. And of course, we could convert all of these into named functions, and it would work just as well. Semantically, as far as all of these examples are concerned, they work exactly the same.

[09:26] Now, things get a little bit different if you start using this, But we're not using this right now. So we'll get into that when we get into objects and stuff Lexical scope. Let's just yeah Anyway, or the lexical this so hopefully that was interesting arrow functions are great. I do use them quite often and I also use function declarations. There's also function expressions which would be basically the same as this except you'd have function and then you wouldn't have this and you would have to have an explicit return.

[09:57] So this is called a function expression. It can actually have a name too, so is even for reels or something. You can give it a specific name here too. This was actually really common years ago before arrow functions were a thing. And now if you're going to do something like this, you may as well just make it an arrow function because it's less characters.

[10:18] So yeah. And especially if you can do an implicit return, like this, which is what we had originally. I keep on typing the wrong character. Holy smokes. Kent, get a hold of yourself.

[10:31] OK. So in any case, you might see some function expressions like that as well. But I prefer either arrow functions or function declarations. Often, I am going to opt for the function declaration unless I am taking advantage of the implicit return features of function expressions or the arrow function expression. Good job on that one.

[10:54] We'll see you in the next.

solution.transcript:
[00:00] Let's jump right into documenting stuff with jsdoc. So we're going to add our jsdoc block. So that is a back or forward slash star star, and then it closes with a star forward slash. It's formatted typically like this where the additional lines have an extra space. Because I don't know.

[00:22] It looks pretty. I always do this. And so first you start out. The top line is the description of what your function does. Our function is an add function that adds two numbers together.

[00:33] And then you have the different parameters. And so these are in order. First parameter is a. So you say param, and then the parameter value. And then you give it a description, the first number, the second number.

[00:45] And then you can say what it returns, the sum of two numbers, and as AI is showing us here, you can also give it an example, which is kind of nice. And so here, what makes this so valuable is that when I'm calling it later, I can say add and look at what I've got here. I've got a number, b number, and it returns a number. Okay, that's nice. The first number is a, adds two numbers together, returns the sum of the two numbers, and here's an example.

[01:14] That's quite nice. That helps a lot in using it. If we didn't have this, then all I would get is anything. That's it. That's all.

[01:25] By having JSDoc, it just really helps in giving something actually really useful for people. Let's do a number together. It's got a parameter a and b, first and second number, returns a sum. Here's an example. So if you're building a library that you're expecting other people to use your functions, you should absolutely be JS docking those functions.

[01:46] OK, let's go ahead and do this one as well. Yep, here we go. We've got our example, everything. We talked about examples already, so that's as much as we're going to talk about there. And then we've got our calculateCompoundInterest.

[01:58] Here's the description. Here are the parameters and what it returns. Here's an example. Pretty much just reps at this point. And then we're gonna make a new function called clamp.

[02:10] And our AI is really good at writing these kinds of functions. So, clamps value between min and max. Here's a value. Here's the min and max. Returns a current value, an example.

[02:20] The JS doc itself can get a little bit more complicated when we're dealing with objects and arrays and stuff like that. So some more advanced types can make this a little bit more complicated. But it is something that your AI agent should be able to handle pretty well for you. And it's pretty sensible once you get used to it. And in fact, you can actually avoid writing .ts files and still get the value of typed JavaScript with .js files by using jsdoc because TypeScript actually understands jsdoc and you can explicitly give a type in the jsdoc for this.

[02:58] So you could say the value is a string or is a number just by using JSDoc, which is pretty cool and some libraries will do that. So you'll run into some libraries that have .js files and they're using JSDoc heavily, and they are still, if they do it right, they are still technically typed and type safe with TypeScript. I don't recommend doing that myself, just use ts everywhere. It runs everywhere except for the browser, but you're going to need a build for the browser anyway. So with that, I think you know everything you need to know about JSDoc.

[03:30] So, great job with this one.

Void And Never.title: Intro to Void And Never
Void And Never.transcript:
[00:00] Okay, in this exercise we're going to talk about void and never. These are... Never is a TypeScript only concept and void is actually both a JavaScript and TypeScript concept. But we're going to be focusing our energy on the TypeScript side of things. So here, let's talk about the void type here really quick.

[00:19] So you can explicitly set your return type to be void. And what that means is there's no return statement, no return at all. And so anybody calling this should not expect to be able to do anything with the result. That's pretty much all that void means. Never type is interesting because it represents a situation that could never possibly happen either because the if statements make it impossible for you to reach this point, like here's a value, it is a number, and then, or it is either this string or this string, and if you checked both of those, then the results, like, it's not possibly anything else, so it's a never type.

[00:58] Or you threw an error, so you can't possibly continue the program, so that's a never type. Or you entered an infinite loop, and so you could never exit. That's another never situation there. And so that can help guide you as you're writing your program, or the agent as it's writing the program for you. So void and never, that's what we're going to talk about in this exercise.

[01:18] We'll see you in the exercise steps.

[00:00] Okay, let's just build out what this has for us. So we're making a logging system. So I'm gonna make a function called log info that simply Logs, but we're gonna have this prefix of info and that that's what makes it a logging system And we're gonna make a log error. [00:36] And then we've got log with timestamp. So we're going to create a timestamp and log that. OK, that's cool. And then we'll log all those, and we'll export them. All right, great.

[00:47] So we've got our info, we've got our error, we've got our timestamp, everything is good. What does this have to do with void? Well, you'll notice that AI actually kind of did this for us and Marty the money bag was here telling you to do this as well, but explicitly typed each one of these as void. What that does is it means that I cannot return anything. I can't return anything except for undefined because undefined is technically the same thing that you would get if we return nothing, if you don't have a return statement.

[01:15] So we're getting a void return here. Now what's kind of interesting is here we have our type is defined as returning void. If I remove that, then our type is actually technically defined as returning undefined. So you can see how these concepts kind of overlap a little bit, but we don't actually need to return undefined. And without that, it's going to say that we returned void.

[01:38] Which effectively, all that really does is it means that I can't do anything to this. Like, It returned nothing. There's nothing that I can call on here. I cannot assign it to a value and have that value have anything on it. There's just nothing that I can do with it.

[01:56] That value is void. It's just nothing. That's why I say we're talking about nothing, because void explicitly, by typing it explicitly or just not returning anything from your function, you're saying just nothing for this. Void is actually not just a TypeScript thing either. Void is a feature of JavaScript.

[02:16] So you can actually say const x equals void 0. That is a fun little trivia here. Even today, we still have bundlers and JavaScript minifiers that try to take the code that you write and make it as small as possible. Use as few characters as possible because it will load faster for whoever's downloading your website. And so we can have this const y equals undefined or we can have const equals void one of these and we can like eliminate all the characters that we possibly can everything one of these is shorter than the other and so actually a minifier is going to go through your code and it's going to find everywhere it finds undefined and it's going to change it for void zero because that is faster.

[03:06] It's not just zero, you can have it be a string, but by adding void to it it just makes this expression undefined regardless of what it returns. You could say date.now, which returns the timestamp for the current date. And without the void, you're going to get number. But if you add a void, it turns that into undefined. Like I said, this isn't something you do a whole lot.

That is something we're going to talk about way later so if that kind of went over your head don't worry about it.

It's good reps and stuff.

[00:24] But yeah, here's what a function that is typed to return never looks like. It's literally just a function that throws an error and that's it. So the reason that it returns never is because it just never returns. There's no situation where calling throwError would result in a value because before it gets to here the error has been thrown. So it's got to evaluate this side first to determine what this value variable should be assigned to, but because we're throwing an error once it hits this line of code it stops execution.

[01:02] It's done. That's what throwing errors does is it's just we're done executing. We're not going to evaluate this side of the equal sign because we stop execution once we hit a throw now we can do try catches and stuff. We'll talk about later, but yeah, throw throwing like this just stops all execution. And that's why value is never.

[01:23] And the value of this is that if I tried to use it, like value, yeah, to fixed or whatever, I'm going to get a typing error here because it's saying, this could never be. It's impossible for you to actually have a value in the value variable. So that's basically never. And here, would you look at that? TypeScript is telling us void.
